text:
a) i) The purpose of the Statement of Financial Position is to provide a snapshot of a company's financial situation at a specific point in time, typically at the end of an accounting period. It presents the company's assets, liabilities, and equity, allowing users to assess its financial health, liquidity, and solvency. The Statement of Financial Position helps stakeholders understand the company's ability to meet its short-term and long-term obligations, as well as its capacity to generate future cash flows.

a) ii) The purpose of the Income Statement is to provide a summary of a company's revenues and expenses over a specific period, typically a month, quarter, or year. It presents the company's net income or loss, which is calculated by subtracting total expenses from total revenues. The Income Statement helps users evaluate a company's profitability, efficiency, and performance over time, allowing them to make informed decisions about investments, lending, or other business activities.

b) i) An expense account represents a cost incurred by a business to generate revenue or maintain its operations. Examples of expense accounts include salaries, rent, utilities, and depreciation. Expense accounts are typically debited, which increases their balance, and are reported on the Income Statement as a deduction from revenue.

b) ii) A liability account represents a debt or obligation that a business owes to its creditors. Examples of liability accounts include accounts payable, loans payable, and taxes owed. Liability accounts are typically credited, which increases their balance, and are reported on the Statement of Financial Position as a claim against the company's assets.

b) iii) An asset account represents a resource owned or controlled by a business, which is expected to provide future economic benefits. Examples of asset accounts include cash, accounts receivable, inventory, and property, plant, and equipment. Asset accounts are typically debited, which increases their balance, and are reported on the Statement of Financial Position as a source of future cash flows.

b) iv) An income account represents a source of revenue earned by a business. Examples of income accounts include sales, service revenue, and interest income. Income accounts are typically credited, which increases their balance, and are reported on the Income Statement as a component of total revenue.

b) v) To correct the omission of the credit note from Diana's accounting records, she should debit the accounts receivable account and credit the sales account by Frw 95,000. This will reduce the accounts receivable balance and the sales revenue, reflecting the correct financial position and performance of the business. The journal entry would be: Debit Accounts Receivable (Frw 95,000), Credit Sales (Frw 95,000).
